title: How One Mom With Brain Injury Fought CA Laws Denying 50K Kids School Access
guestBio: Amy Bohn is the President and Co-Founder of PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids), one of California's most prominent advocates for parental rights and medical freedom. After recovering from a severe brain injury in 2018, she built a grassroots movement of over 30,000 members that has successfully challenged restrictive legislation affecting children's access to education.
excerpt: Amy Bohn couldn't read aloud after her brain injury, but when she discovered 50,000 California kids were denied school access, she built a 30,000-member advocacy movement. Her story proves that one determined mom can challenge an entire system.
metaDescription: Amy Bohn overcame brain injury to lead PERK's fight for 50K California kids denied school access. A mom's journey from recovery to advocacy.
keyTakeaways: Recovery from personal challenges can fuel your capacity to fight for others who need a voice.
Organized parent advocacy can successfully defeat restrictive legislation even in hostile political environments.
Understanding the systematic nature of legislative strategies helps you recognize patterns before they impact your family.
Building community around shared values creates exponential impact beyond what any individual can achieve alone.
Speaking up for vulnerable populations often requires stepping outside your comfort zone and into unfamiliar territory.
Legislative changes in one state often serve as testing grounds for nationwide implementation.
Effective advocacy requires both emotional passion and strategic understanding of political processes.

pullQuotes.text: What started as one exhausted mom showing up at the Capitol became a movement.

timestamps.label: Amy's brain injury and recovery journey
Discovering the impact of SB 277 and SB 276
Building PERK from the ground up
The reality of 50,000 children denied education

content:
Sometimes life breaks you open in ways that prepare you for battles you never imagined fighting. For Amy Bohn, that breaking point came in 2018 with a severe brain injury that left her unable to read a page aloud or walk across a room without exhaustion. But what she discovered during her recovery would ignite a fire that no brain injury could extinguish.

In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Amy shares how she transformed from a struggling recovery patient into the President and Co-Founder of PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids), leading one of California's most effective grassroots movements for parental rights and medical freedom. Her story isn't just about overcoming personal adversity - it's about what happens when a mom refuses to accept that 50,000 children should be denied access to education.

When Recovery Meets Purpose

Amy's journey into advocacy began during her darkest hours. While battling the cognitive and physical challenges of brain injury recovery, she stumbled upon information about California's SB 277 and SB 276 - legislation that was systematically removing medical exemptions and denying children access to schools, daycares, and colleges. The injustice was so glaring that even in her compromised state, she couldn't ignore it.

This is where Amy's story becomes a masterclass in how personal struggle can become the foundation for extraordinary leadership. Instead of using her recovery as a reason to stay small and focus inward, she channeled her healing energy into protecting families who had no voice in the political process. Her brain injury didn't disqualify her from the fight - it qualified her to understand what it means to be vulnerable and need an advocate.

Building a Movement From Ground Zero

What started as one exhausted mom showing up at the Capitol transformed into a sophisticated advocacy organization with over 30,000 members. Under Amy's leadership, PERK has generated more than 5 million emails, phone calls, and measurable actions directed at legislators. But perhaps most impressively, in 2022, her coalition defeated 8 out of 12 extreme bills targeting medical freedom - proving that organized parents can win even in the most challenging political environments.

Amy's approach reveals something crucial about effective leadership: you don't need to be the loudest voice in the room, but you do need to be the most persistent. She understood that sustainable change requires building systems and communities, not just individual heroics. By creating PERK, she multiplied her impact exponentially while providing other parents with the tools and support they needed to become advocates themselves.

The Shocking Reality Behind the Headlines

The statistics Amy shares in this episode should wake up every parent in America. Fifty thousand children in California right now cannot attend school, daycare, or college because of these legislative changes. These aren't abstract policy debates - they represent real families who have been systematically excluded from basic educational access.

What makes Amy's work even more compelling is her ability to explain the step-by-step legislative strategy that created this crisis. She breaks down how SB 277 and SB 276 didn't just happen overnight - they were part of a deliberate, decade-long approach to removing personal belief exemptions, religious freedom, and medical autonomy from California families. Even more concerning, this blueprint is now being replicated in states across the country.

The Intersection of Personal Rights and Community Responsibility

Amy's advocacy touches on fundamental questions about informed consent, parental rights, and the balance between individual choice and collective policies. She references the Nuremberg Code and explains the historical context of medical autonomy in ways that help parents understand what's at stake beyond any single issue.

Her work also illuminates the complex relationship between pharmaceutical liability protection, government mandates, and family decision-making. By explaining the 1986 Act and its implications, Amy helps parents understand the broader systemic issues that affect their ability to make informed healthcare decisions for their children.

Leadership Lessons From the Trenches

Amy's story offers profound insights for any woman who feels called to lead in challenging circumstances. She demonstrates that you don't need to be fully healed, completely prepared, or universally supported to begin making a difference. Sometimes the most authentic leadership emerges from our deepest struggles and most personal experiences with injustice.

Her success with PERK also illustrates the power of building community around shared values rather than trying to go it alone. By creating space for other parents to contribute their skills, resources, and passion, Amy transformed a personal mission into a sustainable movement that continues to grow and adapt.

A Warning and a Call to Action

Perhaps the most important message in this episode is Amy's warning to parents outside of California: what happened here is coming to your state. The legislative strategies, the systematic approach to removing exemptions, and the exclusion of families from the educational system aren't unique to California politics. They're part of a coordinated effort being implemented across the country.

Amy's story proves that ordinary parents can achieve extraordinary results when they're willing to educate themselves, organize strategically, and persist through setbacks. Her journey from brain injury patient to Capitol warrior demonstrates that our most difficult personal experiences often prepare us for our most important contributions to the world.

If you're a parent who has ever been told that bill will never pass or there's nothing you can do about it, Amy's story is your wake-up call. The question isn't whether these changes are coming to your community - it's whether you'll be ready to respond when they do. And Amy Bohn's example shows us that being ready doesn't require perfection, just determination and the courage to speak up for those who need an advocate.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Get your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Advocacy Superpower?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally show up when it's time to fight for what matters most. Amy Bohn's incredible journey from brain injury recovery to leading a movement shows us that advocacy comes in many forms.

questions.question: When you see an injustice happening in your community, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Feel deeply angry and immediately start researching what you can personally do about it
Start conversations with friends and neighbors to see who else cares about this issue
Look into the bigger picture to understand how this fits into larger systemic patterns
Find organizations already working on this and see how you can support their efforts

questions.question: Your biggest personal challenges have taught you:

options.text: That your struggles give you unique insight into what vulnerable people need
How much you need community and how to create it for others going through similar experiences
To recognize warning signs and patterns that others might miss
The importance of showing up consistently, even when you don't feel like the expert

questions.question: When facing an overwhelming problem that affects many people, you're most likely to:

options.text: Channel your outrage into learning everything you can and taking direct action
Focus on bringing the right people together and building collective power
Research the political processes and strategic approaches that could actually work
Look for ways to support the people who are already leading the charge

questions.question: Your approach to stepping outside your comfort zone is:

options.text: When something matters enough, comfort zones become irrelevant
It's easier when you bring other people along for the journey
You prepare thoroughly so the unfamiliar territory feels more manageable
You prefer to support others who are naturally comfortable in those spaces

questions.question: When building momentum for change, your natural strength is:

options.text: Using your personal story and passion to inspire others to care
Creating spaces where people feel connected and empowered to act together
Developing smart strategies and helping others understand the bigger game being played
Providing the consistent energy and support that keeps movements going strong

questions.question: Your leadership style is most effective when:

options.text: You can transform your personal experience into a rallying cry for justice
You're bringing people together around shared values and helping everyone feel heard
You're analyzing complex situations and helping others navigate strategic decisions
You're supporting and amplifying other leaders while ensuring nothing falls through the cracks

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You have an incredible ability to transform personal pain into powerful purpose. Like Amy Bohn, you don't let your own struggles hold you back from fighting for others who need a voice. Your lived experiences become your greatest strength in understanding what vulnerable people need. You lead with both heart and strategy, never underestimating the power of one determined person to create massive change. This episode will inspire you to see how your own challenges might be preparing you for battles you never imagined fighting.

results.title: The Bridge Builder
results.description: You excel at bringing people together around shared values and creating community from chaos. Your superpower lies in seeing connections others miss and building coalitions that amplify everyone's voice. You understand that sustainable change happens through relationships, not just campaigns. You're naturally gifted at helping people feel heard and valued, which makes you incredibly effective at growing movements from the ground up. Amy's story of building PERK will show you how your community-building instincts can create exponential impact beyond what any individual can achieve alone.

results.title: The Strategic Disruptor
results.description: You have a unique gift for seeing patterns and understanding systems that others miss entirely. You're the person who can spot legislative strategies before they impact families and anticipate ripple effects across different environments. Your analytical mind combined with your passion for justice makes you incredibly effective at dismantling unfair systems. You thrive in unfamiliar territory and aren't intimidated by complex political processes. This episode will resonate with your ability to combine emotional passion with strategic thinking to create lasting change.

results.title: The Steady Amplifier
results.description: Your strength lies in consistent, behind-the-scenes work that keeps movements moving forward day after day. You may not always be the loudest voice in the room, but you're often the most reliable one. You excel at supporting others who are ready to step into the spotlight while ensuring the infrastructure exists to sustain their efforts. Your loyalty and dedication create the foundation that allows other advocates to take bigger risks. Amy's journey will inspire you to see how your steady support and amplification of others' voices is absolutely essential to creating the change you want to see.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manage Her, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful intentional leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur and mama of six.

Today's guest is a woman who turned personal challenge into public leadership. Amy Bohn is the president and co-founder of PERK, Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids, an organization that advocates for parental rights, informed consent, and individual freedoms in education and healthcare.

Amy's story began when she moved from Texas to California in 2018, right as new state laws SB277 and SB276 were changing the landscape for parents. At the same time, she was recovering from a serious car accident and brain injury. But instead of stepping back, Amy stepped up, diving head first into advocacy, learning how laws are made, and finding her voice at the Capitol in Sacramento.

In 2020, she became president of PERK. Under her leadership, the organization has grown to more than 30,000 members, leading legislative initiatives and lawsuits around parental rights and medical freedom. Today we're talking about courage, conviction, and what it takes to lead even when life gives you every reason to stay quiet. Welcome to the show, Amy. Thank you for being here.

**Amy:** Thank you, Aimee. I'm so excited to be here. Thank you for having me.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. We've been working on this for a while. I'm finally glad to get you in the studio. So let's start with recovery to leadership. You moved to California at a very pivotal moment. Can you take us back to 2018? What was happening with SB276 at that time and how did that moment intersect with your personal recovery journey?

**Amy:** That's a great question. So first, my husband is from California originally. I'm originally from Arkansas, but we had never lived here together as a family from the time he left California in his college years. He always wanted to come back here. I was like, "Oh, California. I don't know about this. What sounds like a great thing." But in 2018, we were living in Texas and it was the right time and opportunity for a lot of reasons to move to California. So we did that summer.

Lo and behold, little did I know there was this bill called SB277 at the time that had removed all of the personal belief exemptions and religious freedom for families across the whole state. I had no idea that this was happening in California because in Texas everything was still intact when it comes to parental rights and medical freedom. You could have religious exemptions. There was no problem to submit that. So when I got here, I realized there's a fight here. There's something going on that if you didn't live in California, you didn't know or weren't aware of. That really began my journey into the space of health freedom and becoming a leader eventually.

But in 2019 at the beginning of that year, what happened was SB276 was introduced. During I would say those three to four months when we moved to California and we had SB277 and then the introduction of 276, what I saw was everything that mattered to parents, to education, to children, children that were vaccine injured - their rights were being removed and taken away. I couldn't sit silent. I couldn't sleep at night. I'm like, what do I do? How do I help? I don't even know how to go up to a Capitol and talk about any of these issues.

But I started to find my voice one trip at a time, one conversation at a time. I realized that this is one of the most significant battles or issues that I think we all could and need to be aware of and be a part of. So that began my journey into this in 2019.

Like you said, some of the personal stuff. I had been in a car accident right before the summertime in May of 2018 and had a very serious brain injury that I was recovering from. So that was the context of moving. I had an injury before that as well where my nervous system had been damaged because of an injury. I couldn't walk or talk. I couldn't do the normal things that you're supposed to be able to do as a mom or a wife or just a person. That was the context of then the car accident which I then had a brain injury on top of that.

All that being said, I think that taught me that we can't allow life or the things that happen in our life to stop us from what our greatest calling may be or is. Sure, there were some very real realities. I would go up to the Capitol and be exhausted for four days and have to rest for four days after that or for a week at a time. But it mattered so much to me to live and to do it anyway and to really find my voice because during some of these injuries, I literally lost my voice. I couldn't speak.

I remember one time in the process of getting to know what's going on in California and being up at the Capitol, something had been put in my heart on this, which is I understood what it was like to not be able to speak and to be injured to the level where I couldn't communicate what was going on with my body. I connected that to all of these children, to all of these children who had injuries or who had been harmed. They're so little. They didn't have a voice. They literally couldn't communicate.

So I understood at some point in this over the past few years that I could be that voice for the voiceless, for the children who have been harmed. As adults and as people, we can't ever let anything stop us from doing the right thing no matter what it is or how difficult it might be. So that's a little bit of background.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. Amy, you're amazing. You rose up at a time when we needed somebody to rise up into leadership. Could you explain because some of our listeners aren't going to have a clue what SB277 was. Could you explain a little bit about how we got into this battle with some of these bills, how they've layered upon each other, how when they started this battle in California over educational rights for kids, kids' access to school based on medical choices.

**Amy:** Yeah. So actually, I would say this has been a ten-year process, at least a decade. We should actually go back to 2012 because that's when one of the very first laws was introduced and passed. I think it was AB2109 if I remember correctly. But essentially what it did is it required parents to have a physician sign off on them opting out of vaccines or immunizations. So that was the very first brick that was built that intruded into the doctor-patient relationship as well as parents making a decision of what treatments or what things they do for their children. So we started there.

Then you have SB277 which happened in 2015 and that was introduced by the same legislator, the same senator whose name was Senator Richard Pan. That bill went a step further, another brick added to this layer that they were building, which then removed all of the personal belief exemptions from families related to immunizations. So in layman's terms, children could no longer go to school and opt out of vaccinations. Families would be denied religious freedom and personal belief exemptions. So that was 2015.

They didn't stop there. I actually believe, based on a lot of evidence for this, they always had an intention and a plan to bring forward this whole entire removal of parental rights, education access, and medical freedom. They just did it incrementally because they couldn't pass it all at one time in 2012 or in 2015. So they did it one step at a time. But they had this plan. We know that because when the bill was introduced in 2015, they couldn't get it passed with what I'm about to tell you. But they wanted to have IEP and homeschoolers also included in this denial of access to education and personal belief exemptions removed and all of this pressure on parents and families and the medical freedom issue. But they had to remove and amend the bill. So some of those things were left out.

So then what you have is 2015. Then you have 2019 where the same senator introduces the next health freedom catastrophe which was SB276, which then went a whole step further than 2015's bill did and it removed pretty much all of the medical exemptions that children had from 2015 to 2019.

One thing we should mention with 2015 SB277's bill is at the time when they removed all the personal belief exemptions, they said that they would give broad discretion to the doctors and to the physicians to be able to determine what medical exemptions children could have or should have. They could look at family history, medical history, all kinds of things. So there was this blanket umbrella that really protected medical exemptions and doctors being able to choose what's best for children on this issue.

That was then put under a microscope and attacked and removed with SB276 in 2019 because now they started to attack all of the medical exemptions retroactively and punish all the doctors who wrote medical exemptions from 2015 to 2019. The bill they passed successfully, unfortunately, was so awful that it penalized doctors and pretty much removed medical exemptions for almost all children. Currently today, 2025, we have children that their medical exemptions are being revoked based on this bad law. SB276 - pretty much no children can get medical exemptions. They have qualifications in the bill that stated if a child has a coma, they can qualify for a medical exemption. If a child dies, which is horrific to even say that, or there's anaphylactic shock, severe allergic reaction, and nothing else will qualify.

So they're not even honoring what's in the FDA insert that lists so many reactions and injuries and possible contraindications with vaccines. None of those are listed as possible medical exemption qualifications. So that, along with punishing all these doctors, that's what these bills did. They built on them. They built on them. They built on them.

You wish it would stop there in 2019, but it didn't. In 2022, same legislator, Senator Richard Pan, brought forward probably I think five or six atrocious pieces of legislation in 2022. Still on the same topic, same issue - medical freedom, children, children's access to education, parental rights, all of that stuff. He brought forward bills that would make everybody in the whole state of California have a vaccine passport, have to prove verification of their immunization, to work, to travel, to have education. He wanted to push forward a bill that would require the COVID vaccine for all children, zero, babies all the way up to the elderly as a mandate. No choice in this for everyone.

So he was and is in my opinion one of the most extreme legislators that we've ever seen on this issue across the whole country. He brought forward laws that were so extreme it doesn't even account for things that are in the FDA insert, let alone all the harm that's been done. That same senator, I know we kind of talked about this yesterday, but that same senator who termed out in 2022 is now running for Congress for the whole country. I believe that this is my belief based on what's already happened in California that he would like to bring his seriously extreme ideas about medical freedom to the rest of the country for all of the country to potentially lose their medical exemptions except for these few very small criteria, religious freedom, personal belief exemptions.

So I think that this should be a warning for everybody of what happened in California and what could be happening later. That's where PERK was formed. We saw that there was something happening that was unusual. The censorship of parents who were just telling their story. I was a mom. One of my kids is vaccine injured. Went up to the Capitol in 2019 to just try to figure out how you advocate or what you do with this.

As a peaceful protesting mother along with thousands of other parents, they started to create - they meaning the senator, those that were opposing us and our opposition to this bill - they started to say things like we were domestic terrorists as parents. I was there, Aimee. I was there and you were as well at those committee hearings, at those rallies, at those protests. Not one time was any parent or individual acting out of peace. I was just looking back at one of my videos from that time. People were singing Amazing Grace all throughout the hallways. They were protesting. They were advocating for their children. We were going into offices saying this is not right and not okay.

But at no time did any kind of propaganda deserve to be pushed out that tried to paint parents in some way as a terrorist. We weren't. We were using our voice saying this is wrong. It's wrong for you to intrude into our lives. It's wrong for this piece of legislation to punish doctors retroactively. We could see the future that children would be denied education access. These were the very vulnerable. These were less than 1% of the children in the state of California had a medical exemption at the time.

Not to mention now fast forward 2025. There's a big question about autism in our own state, which is why is there 1 in 12 boys - because you can't have half a child, right? But the statistic is that there's 1 in 12 children that have autism now, specifically boys in California. Why? That is versus the national.

**Aimee:** What's the national?

**Amy:** Don't quote me on this if I'm wrong. It's like one in 33 or one in something. Is that about right?

**Aimee:** One in 33.

**Amy:** Something has happened in our state and could it be lots of factors? Absolutely. But is this something we should be looking at as well? Absolutely. If we do not look at this one particular issue, how many vaccines are being mandated at one time in one child's visit over the course of their two-year-old life or five-year-old? I think right now it's like 72 shots are given and more if they do every flu shot and the COVID shot and all these things that are being added to the schedule now.

So that question has to be asked and we cannot be censored. I'm on a soapbox, Aimee.

**Aimee:** Good. I love it.

**Amy:** We can't be censored or marginalized to ask the question that no one's willing to ask. It is a question to be asked and it should be put on the table. If Senator Pan or any of these legislators cannot answer that question with real science instead of silencing everybody, then there's something wrong with that. What are they hiding? What are they afraid of?

Do the study of vaccinated versus unvaccinated in California. Let's look at the databases and see what's going on with that. So there's so much to say about that, but that was the context of what happened brick by brick.

Our role, part of my leadership in California was when 2022 happened, all those other bills that the senator was introducing, that's where we rose up even more and we taught people, everyday people, how to use their voice and make a difference. So we defeated all of those bad bills, like eight out of the twelve. But it wasn't just because of me. It wasn't just because of PERK. It was because we understood that it wasn't actually about us. It was about every organization, every person, every citizen in California, every parent coming together and making sure that our state wasn't lost to all these totalitarian ideas and laws that would have not only removed medical freedom and parental rights and informed consent, but it would have put this tyranny over the state on censorship, on freedom of speech.

One of the things that was introduced as well in 2022 would have allowed any kind of law enforcement - they were going to lose their funding if they didn't enforce what the health department mandates and recommendations were in 2022. So I remember being on a phone call with just the right person at the right time. They had incredible influence in the state legislature on anything related to law enforcement. We brought this information to them. This is what was happening, which was unconstitutional, but legislators don't always care about the constitution or what our rights are.

This particular influencer, he was able to make another phone call being brought this information and kill the bill within a few days. We activated sheriffs. We showed them this is what was happening. That's what it takes to win on these issues - knowing what's happening, educating others about it, and then not just complaining about the atrociousness of it, but teaching and showing people what they can do about it. What they could do about it was send letters, come up to the Capitol with us. And it worked.

We thought outside the box of the how. We had at one point over the summer, we had sent 2.8 million emails in 2022 and over 100,000 tweets and over 100,000 digital postcards out to constituents in these legislators' districts. So we were thinking outside the box of how do we win on these issues because it's so important.

You're right though, the brick by brick. That's what they've done is try to incrementally take away health freedom, medical freedom, children's access to education, which they're being denied right now, by the way. Right now in 2025 there are 50,000 children give or take in the state of California that are not eligible to attend school - private, public - they cannot go to daycare, they cannot go to college. That is the reality right now in California.

I just want this episode to be a warning to women and parents in other states. You must pay attention to legislation. It may seem like a weird and daunting thing to do. I know when this started here in California, I didn't know how a bill was made until it started affecting my life. I had no interest in it at all. I do think this is a warning cry to the parents of the United States to say, "Hey, y'all need to pay attention to the legislation that's getting passed every year in your state. Pay attention because they are slipping little things in that will build into big walls that may affect your life."

**Aimee:** Yeah, that's so true, Amy. The reason why this matters to show people the history of what happened in California and incrementally each of these types of pieces of legislation like you're saying is they have been trying to duplicate what happened here in other states. Hawaii just recently over this past year was trying to remove religious exemptions and personal belief exemptions just like California did. They just did it a few years later. New York, they followed exactly the pattern of what happened in California with that as well. They did successfully remove personal belief exemptions in the state of New York.

We saw Maine, Connecticut had a battle for medical freedom this past year as well. You have Washington, Oregon, a lot of these states where they did try to duplicate what was lost in California, sometimes almost word for word with the same template of legislation that was introduced. So we can't be naive.

I had someone even say just like two days ago, I said California is one of the most important, if not the most important states to keep defending and winning on and securing because what happens here will go to the other states. She was offended. She lived in a different state, okay? A freedom state, okay? She was offended and she's like, "I want to say this kind of offends us." I'm like why would that offend you? The idea was it's a false sense of security. It's that, hey, I live in Texas or I live in Montana, just making a different state up.

She was of the opinion that they've worked so hard to keep their state safe and all that. So how could we say that what's happening in California is going to come to the state near them? That's offensive. This issue, it is offensive to families and people that have lost their rights. But what we're really saying is it can come to your state and it is infiltrating not only the health freedom issues but all the woke stuff that they've passed in California too or keep trying to pass. They're definitely infiltrating that into other states.

Even states like Florida have had school district members that are talking about parental rights are not all-encompassing and can be, I can't remember what I saw. It was something along the lines of parents don't really have all the rights to raise their children. That's in Florida or some of these other states that are freedom or more freedom minded. We need them to stay freedom minded. But if people don't watch what's happening in California and they don't get involved, like you said, Aimee, in the legislative process, understand that you can lose your state. You can lose on these issues if you are complacent or just thinking someone else will win this or save it for you.

**Aimee:** 100%. Because I think that's where I started. I was like, "Oh, no. Someone else will fix this. Someone's going to stop this." I remember my husband saying, "That will never pass. Don't worry, honey. That bill will never pass." I said, "Babe, if I had a dollar for every time I've heard that now." But that's just how it's been.

I do think that as we become the manager, one of the other things that we need to manage as parents and as citizens of our state and of the United States is we have to get involved in the legislative process. You need to stay up to date on what your state laws are being passed from the year. The legislative, there's a period where they're writing laws and you find out when that period is and just do a quick check-in. It's one of those things. It's our civic duty to be involved, right? It really is. We're supposed to be involved in these things. I think we all stepped away from it thinking someone else could handle it for us. I think that's a huge mistake.

**Amy:** I agree with you. I think sometimes what people are missing as well is we the people. That's not just an idea or a phrase that sounds great. It actually is a responsibility. It's a responsibility that every single one of us have to make sure that we the people are the ones that hold accountable these legislators or policy makers or school board members that we voted in. Meaning collectively the people, right?

The whole point of the constitution and our rights is that this country is supposed to be a republic where it's us the people that really can keep our republic or not. If we forget that that's where the power comes from, then that's where we lose. I would say that where we win is what like you were saying when people just wake up. It doesn't have to be, you don't have to start your own organization. You don't have to go out there to every single thing and every single meeting at every single Capitol everything, but you can do something.

Like you said, it could be as simple as follow all the laws going on. Send a letter when you know you need to support or oppose something. Pay attention to what your specific legislator as a constituent or your school district is doing and use your voice. Go to those meetings. If you don't know how to go to a Capitol, you don't know what the process is. Neither did I.

I had, like you brought up, I had a brain injury. Every single thing that I could have very validly used as a reason why not to do this. I was in bed half the time in 2018. I couldn't walk. It was so bad. I'm sharing this not because I'm a victim, because I want people to understand their own power.

I was reading a book when during the brain injury time and I couldn't read a page out loud longer than about one minute a page, couple of paragraphs. I literally could not read it out loud. I couldn't track it. I couldn't focus. My cognitive decline was very real. I was unable to, just my brain was so exhausted after just a little bit of reading out loud, which of course is abnormal. That was one small thing to show and illustrate how severe my injury was.

Even so, even so, I would go up to the Capitol and I would read whatever I could do. I would share something with those legislators. I would use my voice. It didn't matter if I was wiped out for days later or had severe brain injury headaches or none of that mattered because there was too much on the line. I understood that. So really what I kind of hope people see is that we all can do something.

**Aimee:** Yeah.
